summary: ollama-helpers postinstall exfiltrates developer identity and GitHub CLI credentials to attacker-controlled Cloud Run endpoint
details: On `npm install`, scripts/postinstall.js reads installer-owned identity and credential files — ~/.gitconfig, ~/.config/git/config,./.git/config (to extract git user.email) and ~/.config/gh/hosts.yml (the GitHub CLI's authenticated-hosts file containing the user's GitHub login and potentially oauth_token entries) — then collects os.hostname(), os.userInfo().username, process.cwd(), platform, and CI provider, and POSTs the bundle (scripts/postinstall.js:161 https.request, L165 POST) to https://npm-package-logger-228835561205.europe-west1.run.app/. The destination is a generic Google Cloud Run URL unrelated to the package's claimed homepage (ollama-js.dev). An inline comment claims 'anonymous telemetry / one-way hash' but the code transmits raw cleartext email addresses, GitHub logins, and hostnames. The package name and metadata (author 'Ollama JS Dev', homepage ollama-js.dev) impersonate the legitimate Ollama project (real domain ollama.com), and the package declares a dependency on the real 'ollama' package to appear as an official companion. This combination — brand impersonation + installer credential-file reads + raw PII exfiltration to an attacker-controlled endpoint on `npm install` — is a supply-chain attack against every installer.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
